headline: Key Research Findings
subheadline: By-law deep dive, regulatory constraints, and what they mean for the deal
toplineTakeaway: The deal works at $5.5M under both development scenarios — but the pro forma must account for Brookline's inclusionary zoning (15% affordable units), parking requirements, and setback constraints. These reduce upside but don't kill the deal. Meeting with Building Inspector Ted Murray is the critical next step.

keyFindings.title: Section 4.08: Inclusionary Zoning Changes Every Scenario
keyFindings.detail: Brookline requires 15% of units to be Affordable at 80% AMI (for-sale) for any project with 4+ units. This was missing from both Amr's and the original model. For 14 units: 2 affordable units, ~$2.2M revenue reduction. For 5 units: 1 affordable unit or payment-in-lieu, ~$500K-3.2M reduction depending on approach. A payment-in-lieu option exists but the fee amount needs confirmation.
keyFindings.implication: Every pro forma must include this. Payment-in-lieu may be more favorable than on-site units, especially for Beatka's large-unit plan.
keyFindings.source: Brookline Zoning By-Law Section 4.08; Brookline Housing Division

keyFindings.title: Side Setback Formula Is More Restrictive Than Assumed
keyFindings.detail: Emily Noel (Building Inspector) confirmed the side setback = (Building Length / 10) + 10 feet. For an 80-ft building, that's 18 ft per side (36 ft total), vs. the 7.5-10 ft we originally estimated. This significantly constrains the buildable footprint, especially for Scenario A's 14-unit plan which maxes out FAR.
keyFindings.implication: Need lot survey (width x depth) before an architect can confirm whether 14 units physically fit. This is a blocking question for Scenario A.
keyFindings.source: Emily Noel, Building Inspector; Brookline Zoning Table 5.01

keyFindings.title: Transit Overlay Cuts Parking in Half
keyFindings.detail: A 2021 zoning change reduced parking minimums within 0.5 miles of rapid transit to 1.0 space per 1BR+ unit (down from 1.4-2.0). 19 Linden is ~5 min walk from Brookline Village Green Line. This likely qualifies, reducing Scenario A from ~21-28 spaces to ~14 spaces, saving $500K-1M in underground garage costs.
keyFindings.implication: Major cost saving. Confirm Transit Overlay boundary includes the parcel.
keyFindings.source: Brookline Zoning By-Law Section 6.02; 2021 Transit Overlay amendment

keyFindings.title: Section 5.21 Could Unlock FAR/Height Bonus
keyFindings.detail: Public benefit incentive allows exceptions to FAR and height limits. Historic preservation of the Greek Revival house could qualify. If activated, this could allow additional sellable sqft above the 17,698 FAR cap — particularly valuable for Scenario A which is at max FAR (actually 162 sqft over).
keyFindings.implication: Potential upside. Discuss with Ted Murray whether this applies.

keyFindings.title: Keeping the House Avoids the 18-Month Demo Delay
keyFindings.detail: Both Amr and Beatka's plans preserve the existing house. The building inspector confirmed the 18-month delay 'may be taken into effect' — but it only applies to demolition. Adaptive reuse = no demolition = no delay. This saves ~$660K in carrying costs and 18 months of timeline.
keyFindings.implication: The strongest argument for adaptive reuse over full demolition, beyond community goodwill
keyFindings.source: Emily Noel; Brookline Demolition Delay By-Law Article 5.3

keyFindings.title: Density Formula Allows Up to 15 Units
keyFindings.detail: Confirmed: 3,000 sqft lot area for first unit + 1,000 sqft each additional. On 17,698 sqft lot: max 15 units (1 + 14 additional). Amr's 14-unit plan is 1 under the max. Beatka's 5-unit plan uses only a third of the allowed density.
keyFindings.implication: Amr is near the density ceiling; Beatka has significant headroom for future phase or if regulations change
keyFindings.source: Brookline Zoning By-Law; Amr's analysis confirmed by Building Inspector guidance

keyFindings.title: FAR Overage Must Be Fixed (Scenario A)
keyFindings.detail: Amr's model totals 17,860 sqft (16,200 living + 1,660 common) on a 17,698 sqft lot. That's 162 sqft over the FAR 1.0 limit. Small overage but it's a hard legal cap — must be trimmed.
keyFindings.implication: Not a deal-breaker. Reduce common space from 1,660 to ~1,500 sqft or trim unit sizes slightly.

keyFindings.title: Two Credible Scenarios Converge on Key Decisions
keyFindings.detail: Amr (14 units, $23M) and Beatka (5-6 units, $20M) disagree on density but agree on three critical points: (1) keep the existing house, (2) underground garage, (3) avoid Chapter 40B. This convergence from independent sources is a strong signal.
keyFindings.implication: The adaptive reuse + new construction approach is validated from both financial and market perspectives

keyFindings.title: Small Units Command a Significant Premium
keyFindings.detail: Amr prices 1,000 sqft units at $1,500/sqft while existing house units price at $1,200/sqft. Comp support: 58 Kent St (1,217 sqft) sold at $1,396/sqft. Solaire Fisher Hill (3,000-4,000 sqft) at $1,000-1,333/sqft. The $/sqft premium for smaller units is real and documented.
keyFindings.implication: Scenario A's revenue advantage is real — but $1,500/sqft is at the top of market. $1,200-1,350 is the defensible range.

keyFindings.title: Building Inspector Has Laid Out a Roadmap
keyFindings.detail: Emily Noel identified the exact by-law sections to review (4.08, 5.21, 6) and connected us with Ted Murray, the area inspector. She recommended meeting with him and the preservation department before design. This is a clear, actionable regulatory pathway.
keyFindings.implication: The regulatory path is navigable. Meeting with Ted Murray should resolve most open questions in a single session.
keyFindings.source: Emily Noel, Building Inspector (emurray@brooklinema.gov for Ted Murray)

keyFindings.title: Rear Setback (30 ft) and Height (40 ft) Are Confirmed
keyFindings.detail: M-1.0 requires 30 ft rear yard setback and allows 40 ft max height (4 stories). Combined with front setback (10-20 ft), you lose 40-50 ft of lot depth. 4 stories is the right design target to keep footprint manageable at ~4,425 sqft per floor.
keyFindings.implication: Architect should design for 4 stories to work within setback constraints

keyFindings.title: The Existing House Split Needs Vetting
keyFindings.detail: Amr's model assumes the Greek Revival house becomes 2 units (3,000 + 1,200 sqft). But where does the second unit come from? If interior conversion requires significant exterior changes, it could trigger the 'systematic removal of character-defining elements' clause and activate the demolition delay even while preserving the building.
keyFindings.implication: Confirm with preservation department. If the split is infeasible, the house stays as 1 unit (reducing total unit count to 13 and adjusting revenue by ~$1.4M).
keyFindings.source: Brookline Demolition Delay By-Law; analysis of Amr's unit mix

scenarios.condition: Scenario A (14 units) at $5.5M, new units at $1,500/sqft, with IZ
scenarios.reasoning: 25.4% margin, $5.3M profit. Strong returns even after IZ adjustment. Requires $1,500/sqft to hold and buildable envelope to accommodate 14 units.

scenarios.condition: Scenario A (14 units) at $5.5M, new units at $1,200/sqft, with IZ
scenarios.reasoning: 12.9% margin, $2.3M profit. Acceptable but thin for 4-year execution risk. Works if you can control costs tightly.

scenarios.condition: Scenario B (5 units) at $5.5M, blended $1,200/sqft, payment-in-lieu IZ
scenarios.reasoning: ~25% margin, ~$5M profit. Lower risk, simpler execution, faster timeline. Best risk-adjusted path if payment-in-lieu is available.

scenarios.condition: Scenario B (5 units) at $5.5M, blended $1,200/sqft, on-site affordable unit
scenarios.reasoning: 14% margin, $2.6M profit. Still profitable but the single affordable unit loses more revenue on large-unit plans. Negotiate hard on payment-in-lieu.
Both scenarios still work at asking price if pricing holds — but every dollar saved on acquisition flows directly to margin. $5.5M is the target.

scenarios.condition: Setback analysis shows 14 units don't physically fit
scenarios.reasoning: If the lot geometry can't support 14 units, Beatka's 5-6 unit plan becomes the clear path. Still produces strong returns.
